text_summary: The NCCN Clinical Practice Guidelines for Non-Small Cell Lung Cancer (Version 3.2025) provide comprehensive recommendations for molecular testing, systemic therapy, and sequencing in advanced NSCLC. All patients with advanced non-squamous NSCLC and select squamous cell patients should undergo broad molecular profiling including EGFR, ALK, ROS1, BRAF V600E, NTRK, MET exon 14, RET, KRAS G12C, ERBB2, PD-L1, and emerging biomarkers. First-line therapy depends on driver mutation status: osimertinib for EGFR-mutant, alectinib or lorlatinib for ALK-positive, pembrolizumab monotherapy for PD-L1>=50% without drivers, and pembrolizumab plus chemotherapy for PD-L1<50% without drivers. The guidelines emphasize that biomarker results should be available before initiating systemic therapy whenever possible, and that liquid biopsy is acceptable when tissue is insufficient.
key_recommendations: Broad molecular profiling mandatory for all advanced non-squamous NSCLC. Osimertinib preferred 1L for EGFR-mutant. Alectinib or lorlatinib for ALK-positive. Pembrolizumab monotherapy for PD-L1>=50% without drivers. Sotorasib or adagrasib for KRAS G12C after prior therapy. Selpercatinib for RET fusion. Capmatinib or tepotinib for MET exon 14 skip.
molecular_testing: EGFR, ALK, ROS1, BRAF V600E, NTRK, MET exon 14, RET, KRAS G12C, ERBB2, PD-L1 IHC, consider TMB and HER2 mutations

text_summary: The NCCN Guidelines for Breast Cancer (Version 2.2025) mandate ER, PR, and HER2 testing on all invasive breast cancers. For HR-positive/HER2-negative metastatic breast cancer, CDK4/6 inhibitor plus endocrine therapy is the standard first-line treatment. Oncotype DX or MammaPrint should guide adjuvant chemotherapy decisions in node-negative or node-limited HR+/HER2- early-stage disease. For HER2-positive metastatic disease, trastuzumab deruxtecan has become the preferred second-line agent based on DESTINY-Breast03. HER2-low (IHC 1+ or IHC 2+/FISH-negative) is now a distinct actionable category qualifying for trastuzumab deruxtecan. For TNBC, pembrolizumab plus chemotherapy is the standard neoadjuvant regimen (KEYNOTE-522), and sacituzumab govitecan is preferred for pretreated metastatic TNBC. Germline BRCA testing is recommended for all patients meeting NCCN criteria, with PARP inhibitors available for BRCA-mutant disease.
key_recommendations: ER/PR/HER2 testing mandatory. CDK4/6i + endocrine therapy for HR+/HER2- metastatic (1L). T-DXd preferred 2L for HER2-positive. T-DXd for HER2-low after one prior chemotherapy. Sacituzumab govitecan for pretreated TNBC. Pembrolizumab + chemo for early TNBC. PARP inhibitors for germline BRCA-mutant. Alpelisib for PIK3CA-mutant HR+ disease.
molecular_testing: ER, PR, HER2 IHC/FISH, Oncotype DX or MammaPrint (early-stage HR+), germline BRCA1/2, PIK3CA, ESR1 (ctDNA at progression), PD-L1 (TNBC)

text_summary: The NCCN Guidelines for Colon and Rectal Cancer (Version 1.2025) recommend universal molecular testing for all metastatic CRC patients, including RAS (KRAS/NRAS exons 2-4), BRAF V600E, MSI/MMR status, HER2, and NTRK fusions. First-line pembrolizumab is the preferred option for MSI-H/dMMR metastatic CRC (KEYNOTE-177). For RAS/BRAF wild-type, anti-EGFR therapy (cetuximab or panitumumab) combined with chemotherapy is the preferred left-sided approach. BRAF V600E-mutant CRC has poor prognosis and is treated with encorafenib plus cetuximab (BEACON trial) in the second-line setting. Adjuvant therapy decisions for stage II MSI-H CRC favor observation over fluoropyrimidine chemotherapy due to the favorable prognostic impact of MSI-H. ctDNA-guided adjuvant therapy decisions are being investigated in the CIRCULATE and DYNAMIC trials.
key_recommendations: Universal RAS/BRAF/MSI testing for mCRC. Pembrolizumab first-line for MSI-H mCRC. Anti-EGFR + chemo for RAS/BRAF-WT left-sided. Encorafenib + cetuximab for BRAF V600E (2L). FOLFOXIRI + bevacizumab for aggressive biology. Stage II MSI-H may omit adjuvant chemo. ctDNA for MRD assessment under investigation.
molecular_testing: KRAS/NRAS exons 2-4, BRAF V600E, MSI/MMR (universal), HER2 amplification, NTRK fusions, tumor sidedness

text_summary: The NCCN Guidelines for Melanoma (Version 1.2025) recommend BRAF V600 mutation testing for all patients with unresectable stage III or stage IV melanoma. First-line treatment options include combination immunotherapy (nivolumab plus ipilimumab or nivolumab plus relatlimab) or anti-PD-1 monotherapy (pembrolizumab, nivolumab). For BRAF V600-mutant melanoma, BRAF+MEK inhibitor combinations (dabrafenib+trametinib, encorafenib+binimetinib, vemurafenib+cobimetinib) are alternatives to immunotherapy, though immunotherapy is generally preferred first-line due to durability of response. The adjuvant setting includes pembrolizumab (KEYNOTE-054) and nivolumab (CheckMate-238) for resected stage III/IV. Neoadjuvant pembrolizumab plus ipilimumab is emerging for resectable stage III disease. KIT mutations in mucosal and acral melanomas may be targeted with imatinib. Gene expression profiling (DecisionDx-Melanoma) can aid staging decisions.
key_recommendations: BRAF V600 testing for all unresectable/metastatic. Nivolumab + ipilimumab or anti-PD-1 preferred 1L. BRAF+MEK combination for BRAF-mutant as alternative. Adjuvant pembrolizumab or nivolumab for resected stage III/IV. KIT testing for mucosal/acral subtypes. Neoadjuvant immunotherapy emerging for resectable stage III.
molecular_testing: BRAF V600, KIT (acral/mucosal), NRAS, gene expression profiling (DecisionDx), PD-L1 (less established utility), TMB

text_summary: The NCCN Guidelines for Pancreatic Adenocarcinoma (Version 1.2025) recommend molecular profiling for all patients with metastatic disease to identify actionable alterations including BRCA1/2, PALB2, MSI-H/dMMR, NTRK fusions, TMB-H, KRAS (for emerging G12C therapies), and HER2 amplification. Germline testing is recommended for all pancreatic cancer patients regardless of family history. For patients with germline BRCA-mutant metastatic disease responding to platinum-based chemotherapy, olaparib maintenance is recommended based on the POLO trial. First-line chemotherapy options include FOLFIRINOX (for fit patients) and gemcitabine plus nab-paclitaxel. Pembrolizumab is recommended for MSI-H/dMMR tumors. The guidelines acknowledge the aggressive biology and limited targeted therapy options in pancreatic cancer but emphasize the importance of molecular profiling to identify the approximately 5-10% with actionable alterations.
key_recommendations: Molecular profiling for all metastatic pancreatic cancer. Germline testing recommended for all patients. Olaparib maintenance for germline BRCA-mutant after platinum response (POLO trial). FOLFIRINOX or gemcitabine + nab-paclitaxel as first-line. Pembrolizumab for MSI-H. Larotrectinib/entrectinib for NTRK fusions.
molecular_testing: BRCA1/2 (germline + somatic), PALB2, MSI/MMR, NTRK fusions, TMB, KRAS, HER2, comprehensive genomic profiling recommended

text_summary: The NCCN Guidelines for Ovarian Cancer (Version 1.2025) strongly recommend BRCA1/2 germline and somatic testing and HRD testing for all patients with epithelial ovarian cancer. PARP inhibitor maintenance therapy is a major component of frontline treatment: olaparib for BRCA-mutant (SOLO-1: PFS 56 months), niraparib for all-comers in first-line maintenance, and olaparib plus bevacizumab for HRD-positive tumors. In the recurrent setting, PARP inhibitor selection depends on prior PARP exposure, platinum sensitivity, and BRCA/HRD status. Pembrolizumab is recommended for MSI-H/dMMR ovarian cancer. The guidelines emphasize that all patients should undergo genetic counseling and germline testing, as Lynch syndrome and BRCA-related hereditary cancer syndromes are identified in approximately 15-20% of ovarian cancer patients. Mirvetuximab soravtansine is approved for FRalpha-positive, platinum-resistant ovarian cancer.
key_recommendations: BRCA1/2 and HRD testing for all epithelial ovarian cancer. PARP inhibitor maintenance is standard after first-line platinum response. Olaparib for BRCA-mutant. Niraparib for all-comers maintenance. Olaparib + bevacizumab for HRD-positive. Mirvetuximab soravtansine for FRalpha+ platinum-resistant. Germline genetic testing and counseling for all patients.
molecular_testing: BRCA1/2 (germline + somatic), HRD score (myChoice CDx), MSI/MMR, NTRK fusions, folate receptor alpha (IHC), comprehensive molecular profiling

text_summary: The NCCN Guidelines for Prostate Cancer (Version 1.2025) recommend germline testing for all patients with metastatic prostate cancer and somatic tumor sequencing for metastatic castration-resistant prostate cancer (mCRPC). BRCA2 mutations are found in approximately 5-7% of mCRPC and confer sensitivity to PARP inhibitors. Olaparib is approved for BRCA1/2-mutant mCRPC (PROfound trial). MSI-H/dMMR status qualifies for pembrolizumab (approximately 3% of mCRPC). The treatment landscape for mCRPC includes androgen receptor pathway inhibitors (enzalutamide, abiraterone, darolutamide, apalutamide), PARP inhibitors for DNA repair-deficient tumors, pembrolizumab for MSI-H, lutetium-177 PSMA for PSMA-positive disease, and novel combinations. The TALAPRO-2 and PROpel trials investigated PARP + novel hormonal agent combinations in broader mCRPC populations.
key_recommendations: Germline testing for all metastatic prostate cancer. Somatic testing for mCRPC. Olaparib for BRCA-mutant mCRPC. Pembrolizumab for MSI-H mCRPC. Lutetium-177 PSMA for PSMA-positive mCRPC after ARPI and taxane. Enzalutamide/abiraterone/darolutamide for mCRPC. Genetic counseling for HRR gene mutation carriers.
molecular_testing: BRCA1/2, ATM, PALB2, HRR gene panel (somatic), MSI/MMR, PSMA PET/CT, germline multigene panel

text_summary: The NCCN Guidelines for Kidney Cancer (Version 2.2025) recommend immune checkpoint combination regimens as first-line therapy for advanced clear cell RCC. Preferred regimens include pembrolizumab plus lenvatinib (CLEAR trial: PFS 23.9 months), nivolumab plus ipilimumab for intermediate/poor-risk (CheckMate-214), pembrolizumab plus axitinib (KEYNOTE-426), and nivolumab plus cabozantinib (CheckMate-9ER). Cabozantinib monotherapy remains an option for IMDC favorable-risk patients. The HIF-2alpha inhibitor belzutifan is approved for VHL disease-associated RCC and is being studied in sporadic clear cell RCC. Molecular profiling is recommended for non-clear cell histologies to identify potential actionable targets. The guidelines acknowledge that VHL loss drives VEGF and HIF-2alpha signaling in clear cell RCC, providing the biological rationale for anti-VEGF strategies. Biomarkers for immunotherapy selection in RCC remain an unmet need.
key_recommendations: IO combination first-line for advanced clear cell RCC. Pembrolizumab + lenvatinib preferred. Nivolumab + ipilimumab for intermediate/poor risk. Belzutifan for VHL disease-associated RCC. Cabozantinib for favorable risk or after IO. Molecular profiling for non-clear cell. Active surveillance for small renal masses.
molecular_testing: VHL (germline for VHL disease), molecular profiling for non-clear cell histologies, PD-L1 (not routinely used for treatment decisions)

text_summary: The NCCN Guidelines for Bladder Cancer (Version 2.2025) recommend molecular profiling for advanced urothelial carcinoma, including FGFR alterations, HER2 status, NTRK fusions, and MSI/TMB. Erdafitinib is approved for FGFR2/3-altered metastatic urothelial carcinoma. Enfortumab vedotin plus pembrolizumab has become the preferred first-line regimen for advanced urothelial carcinoma based on the EV-302/KEYNOTE-A39 trial (PFS 12.5 months, OS not reached). Avelumab maintenance after platinum-based chemotherapy (JAVELIN Bladder 100) is recommended for patients not eligible for first-line EV+pembrolizumab. Sacituzumab govitecan is an option for pretreated urothelial carcinoma. PD-L1 testing guides atezolizumab and pembrolizumab eligibility in cisplatin-ineligible patients. The guidelines emphasize the rapidly evolving treatment landscape in urothelial carcinoma with antibody-drug conjugates and biomarker-directed therapies.
key_recommendations: Enfortumab vedotin + pembrolizumab preferred 1L for advanced urothelial. Erdafitinib for FGFR2/3-altered. Avelumab maintenance after platinum. Sacituzumab govitecan for pretreated disease. PD-L1 testing for cisplatin-ineligible patients. FGFR testing recommended for all advanced urothelial.
molecular_testing: FGFR2/3 alterations, PD-L1 (CPS), HER2 (Nectin-4 for EV eligibility), NTRK fusions, MSI/MMR, TMB, comprehensive genomic profiling

text_summary: The NCCN Guidelines for Head and Neck Cancers (Version 1.2025) recommend PD-L1 testing (CPS score) for recurrent or metastatic head and neck squamous cell carcinoma (R/M HNSCC) to guide first-line immunotherapy decisions. Pembrolizumab plus chemotherapy is the preferred first-line regimen for CPS>=1 (KEYNOTE-048: OS 13.0 months). For CPS>=20, pembrolizumab monotherapy is an alternative first-line option. HPV/p16 status is essential for oropharyngeal cancer staging and prognosis but does not currently change systemic therapy selection in the R/M setting. The NCCN emphasizes the importance of multidisciplinary tumor board review, particularly for treatment selection in locally advanced disease. Cetuximab remains an option in combination with platinum/5-FU for PD-L1-negative R/M HNSCC. Comprehensive genomic profiling may identify NTRK fusions, HRAS mutations, and other actionable alterations in a minority of patients.
key_recommendations: PD-L1 CPS testing for all R/M HNSCC. Pembrolizumab + chemo for CPS>=1 (1L). Pembrolizumab monotherapy for CPS>=20. HPV/p16 testing for oropharyngeal cancer staging. Cetuximab + chemo alternative for PD-L1-negative. Comprehensive profiling may identify actionable targets. Multidisciplinary tumor board review.
molecular_testing: PD-L1 (CPS with 22C3), HPV/p16 (oropharyngeal), comprehensive genomic profiling (consider NTRK, HRAS, PIK3CA)

text_summary: The NCCN Guidelines for Acute Myeloid Leukemia (Version 1.2025) recommend rapid comprehensive molecular profiling at diagnosis, including FLT3-ITD, FLT3-TKD, NPM1, IDH1, IDH2, TP53, CEBPA, RUNX1, ASXL1, KIT, and cytogenetics. FLT3-ITD mutant AML receives midostaurin added to 7+3 induction (RATIFY trial) and gilteritinib for relapsed/refractory disease. IDH1-mutant AML is treated with ivosidenib (with or without azacitidine). IDH2-mutant AML with enasidenib. For patients unfit for intensive chemotherapy, venetoclax plus azacitidine has become the standard of care with significantly improved OS. The 2022 ELN risk classification integrates molecular markers into favorable/intermediate/adverse risk groups. TP53-mutant AML has the worst prognosis and limited effective therapies. Measurable residual disease (MRD) monitoring by multiparameter flow cytometry or molecular methods is recommended for treatment response assessment.
key_recommendations: Rapid molecular profiling at diagnosis mandatory. FLT3-ITD: midostaurin + 7+3, gilteritinib for R/R. IDH1-mutant: ivosidenib. IDH2-mutant: enasidenib. Venetoclax + azacitidine for unfit patients. ELN 2022 risk classification. MRD monitoring recommended. TP53-mutant: clinical trial preferred.
molecular_testing: FLT3-ITD/TKD, NPM1, IDH1, IDH2, TP53, CEBPA, RUNX1, ASXL1, KIT, cytogenetics/FISH, MRD by flow or molecular

text_summary: The NCCN Guidelines for Chronic Myeloid Leukemia (Version 1.2025) recommend BCR-ABL1 quantitative PCR monitoring as the cornerstone of CML management. First-line therapy with imatinib, dasatinib, nilotinib, or bosutinib is selected based on CML risk score (Sokal, EUTOS), comorbidities, and adverse-event profile. Deep molecular response (MR4, BCR-ABL1 <=0.01% IS) after sustained TKI therapy may allow treatment-free remission (TFR) attempts. ABL1 kinase domain mutation analysis is recommended at treatment failure or suboptimal response to guide second-line TKI selection. The T315I gatekeeper mutation is resistant to all first/second-generation TKIs but sensitive to ponatinib and asciminib. Asciminib, a STAMP (Specifically Targeting the ABL Myristoyl Pocket) inhibitor, represents a novel mechanism for CML treatment. The guidelines emphasize milestone-based response assessment at 3, 6, 12, and 18 months using BCR-ABL1 IS quantification.
key_recommendations: TKI therapy for all CML-CP patients. BCR-ABL1 monitoring every 3 months. Milestone responses at 3/6/12/18 months. ABL1 mutation analysis at failure. Ponatinib or asciminib for T315I. Treatment-free remission possible after sustained MR4. Second-line based on mutation profile and comorbidities.
molecular_testing: BCR-ABL1 quantitative RT-PCR (IS scale), ABL1 kinase domain mutation analysis, cytogenetics at diagnosis

text_summary: ASCO Provisional Clinical Opinion on Immunotherapy Biomarkers (2025 update) recommends PD-L1 IHC testing for NSCLC, urothelial carcinoma, HNSCC, gastric/GEJ, cervical, and TNBC to guide anti-PD-1/PD-L1 therapy. MSI/MMR testing is recommended in colorectal, endometrial, gastric, and other GI cancers, with tissue-agnostic applicability for MSI-H/dMMR. TMB assessment by validated CGP panels is recommended for tumors without other biomarker-directed options. The opinion acknowledges that PD-L1 has significant limitations (dynamic expression, assay discordance, imperfect predictive value) and that combination biomarker strategies may improve patient selection. Emerging biomarkers under evaluation include tumor-infiltrating lymphocytes, interferon-gamma gene signatures, ctDNA dynamics, and gut microbiome composition. ASCO emphasizes that absence of PD-L1 expression does not preclude immunotherapy benefit in many contexts, particularly with combination regimens.
key_recommendations: PD-L1 testing recommended for NSCLC, urothelial, HNSCC, gastric, cervical, TNBC. MSI/MMR universal in CRC and endometrial. TMB by validated CGP for biomarker-unselected tumors. Combination biomarkers may improve selection. PD-L1 absence does not preclude IO benefit with combinations. ctDNA dynamics as emerging response biomarker.
molecular_testing: PD-L1 IHC (assay-specific: 22C3, 28-8, SP142, SP263), MSI by PCR/NGS or MMR by IHC, TMB by CGP, ctDNA (emerging)

text_summary: The ASCO Precision Medicine Position Statement (2024 update) endorses routine somatic tumor profiling for all patients with advanced/metastatic cancer to identify actionable genomic alterations. ASCO recommends NGS-based comprehensive genomic profiling over single-gene testing due to the expanding landscape of targetable alterations and tissue-agnostic approvals. Key principles include: germline incidental findings require genetic counseling, molecular tumor boards should review complex cases, clinical trial matching should be integrated into profiling workflows, and evidence-based resources (OncoKB, CIViC, ESCAT) should guide variant interpretation. ASCO acknowledges challenges including turnaround time, cost-effectiveness, and the gap between variant identification and available targeted therapies. The statement emphasizes equitable access to molecular profiling across healthcare settings and supports development of pan-cancer molecular databases for outcomes tracking.
key_recommendations: NGS-based CGP recommended over single-gene testing for advanced cancers. Germline findings require genetic counseling. Molecular tumor boards for complex cases. Clinical trial matching integrated with profiling. OncoKB/CIViC for variant interpretation. Equitable access to profiling. Pan-cancer outcomes databases needed.
molecular_testing: Comprehensive genomic profiling (NGS), liquid biopsy when tissue unavailable, germline testing when indicated, RNA-based sequencing for fusions

text_summary: The ESMO Scale for Clinical Actionability of Molecular Targets (ESCAT, 2024 update) provides a tiered framework for classifying the clinical utility of genomic alterations in oncology. Tier I: alteration-drug match validated in clinical trials (EGFR mutations in NSCLC, HER2 amplification in breast). Tier II: investigational biomarker in clinical trials. Tier III: clinical benefit in another tumor type (off-label). Tier IV: preclinical evidence only. ESMO Precision Medicine Working Group recommends next-generation sequencing for all patients with advanced cancer, with specific recommendations by tumor type. The ESMO guidelines emphasize the distinction between molecular targets with demonstrated clinical utility (ESCAT I) versus those with only biological rationale (ESCAT IV). ESMO recommends molecular tumor boards for interpretation of complex molecular profiles and alignment of treatment recommendations with ESCAT levels. The framework enables cross-country harmonization of precision medicine access.
key_recommendations: ESCAT tier system: I (validated), II (investigational), III (off-label evidence), IV (preclinical). NGS recommended for all advanced cancers. Molecular tumor boards for complex profiles. Tier I alterations warrant standard treatment. Tier II-III warrant clinical trial consideration. Cross-tumor-type evidence considered for tissue-agnostic targets. ESCAT levels guide reimbursement decisions in EU.
molecular_testing: Comprehensive NGS (DNA + RNA), tumor type-specific panels minimum, whole-exome/genome sequencing for research settings

text_summary: The NCCN Guidelines for Bladder/Urothelial Cancer (Version 3.2025) reflect the transformative shift in first-line treatment with enfortumab vedotin plus pembrolizumab becoming the preferred regimen for locally advanced or metastatic urothelial carcinoma based on EV-302/KEYNOTE-A39 (PFS 12.5 months, OS not reached). Gemcitabine plus cisplatin or carboplatin remains an alternative, followed by avelumab maintenance for those with stable or responding disease (JAVELIN Bladder 100). Erdafitinib is recommended for FGFR2/3-altered disease. Sacituzumab govitecan and single-agent pembrolizumab or nivolumab are options for later lines. Molecular testing should include FGFR2/3 alterations, PD-L1, MSI/MMR, TMB, and NTRK fusions. The guidelines emphasize the importance of FGFR testing at diagnosis given the availability of erdafitinib. Radical cystectomy with pelvic lymph node dissection remains the standard for muscle-invasive disease with neoadjuvant cisplatin-based chemotherapy recommended for eligible patients.
key_recommendations: Enfortumab vedotin + pembrolizumab preferred 1L for advanced urothelial. Gem/cis or gem/carbo alternative 1L, followed by avelumab maintenance. Erdafitinib for FGFR2/3-altered. Sacituzumab govitecan for pretreated. FGFR, PD-L1, MSI testing recommended. Neoadjuvant cisplatin-based chemo for MIBC.
molecular_testing: FGFR2/3 alterations (mandatory), PD-L1 CPS, MSI/MMR, TMB, NTRK fusions, HER2, comprehensive genomic profiling

text_summary: The NCCN Guidelines for Gastric Cancer (Version 2.2025) incorporate zolbetuximab for CLDN18.2-positive, HER2-negative advanced gastric/GEJ adenocarcinoma based on the SPOTLIGHT and GLOW trials. HER2 testing by IHC/FISH is mandatory for all advanced gastric cancers, with trastuzumab plus chemotherapy remaining the standard for HER2-positive disease. PD-L1 CPS testing guides the addition of nivolumab to chemotherapy (CheckMate-649: CPS>=5 population). MSI-H/dMMR testing is recommended for all patients. Molecular profiling should include CLDN18.2 (IHC), HER2, PD-L1, MSI/MMR, and consideration of NTRK fusions and TMB. First-line for HER2-negative, CLDN18.2-negative disease is fluoropyrimidine/platinum plus nivolumab for CPS>=5. The guidelines emphasize that CLDN18.2 and HER2 testing should be performed concurrently at diagnosis to avoid treatment delays. Perioperative FLOT chemotherapy remains the standard for resectable gastric cancer.
key_recommendations: HER2 testing mandatory for all advanced gastric. Trastuzumab + chemo for HER2-positive. Zolbetuximab + chemo for CLDN18.2-positive/HER2-negative. Nivolumab + chemo for CPS>=5. MSI testing universal. CLDN18.2 IHC at diagnosis. Perioperative FLOT for resectable disease.
molecular_testing: HER2 IHC/FISH, CLDN18.2 IHC (VENTANA 43-14A), PD-L1 CPS, MSI/MMR, NTRK fusions, TMB, comprehensive profiling for advanced disease

text_summary: The NCCN Guidelines for Ovarian Cancer (Version 2.2025) reinforce BRCA1/2 and HRD testing for all epithelial ovarian cancers and add FRalpha testing for patients with platinum-resistant disease. PARP inhibitor maintenance remains the standard: olaparib for BRCA-mutant after first-line platinum response (SOLO-1), niraparib for HRD-positive (PRIMA), and olaparib plus bevacizumab for HRD-positive with residual disease. Mirvetuximab soravtansine is now a preferred option for FRalpha-positive, platinum-resistant ovarian cancer based on the MIRASOL trial (OS benefit: 16.5 vs 12.8 months). Pembrolizumab is recommended for MSI-H/dMMR ovarian cancers. Genetic counseling and germline testing are recommended for all patients regardless of family history. The guidelines emphasize comprehensive molecular profiling including BRCA1/2 (germline and somatic), HRD score, MSI/MMR, FRalpha (IHC), and NTRK fusions. Bevacizumab is incorporated into multiple lines of therapy.
key_recommendations: BRCA/HRD testing for all epithelial ovarian. PARP maintenance standard after 1L platinum response. FRalpha testing for platinum-resistant disease. Mirvetuximab soravtansine for FRalpha-positive platinum-resistant. Pembrolizumab for MSI-H. Germline testing for all patients. Bevacizumab in multiple settings.
molecular_testing: BRCA1/2 (germline + somatic), HRD score (myChoice CDx), MSI/MMR, FRalpha IHC (VENTANA FOLR1 26B3), NTRK fusions, comprehensive molecular profiling

text_summary: The NCCN Guidelines for Small Cell Lung Cancer (Version 3.2025) incorporate tarlatamab as a new treatment option for previously treated extensive-stage SCLC based on the DeLLphi-301 trial. First-line treatment remains platinum-etoposide plus atezolizumab (IMpower133) or durvalumab (CASPIAN). Tarlatamab (DLL3-targeting BiTE antibody) is now listed for second-line or subsequent therapy, providing the first bispecific T-cell engager option in SCLC. Lurbinectedin remains an option for previously treated SCLC. Topotecan is an alternative second-line agent. The guidelines recommend CRS prophylaxis and monitoring during tarlatamab initiation, including step-up dosing and hospitalization for initial cycles. Brain MRI surveillance is important given the high rate of CNS metastases in SCLC. Prophylactic cranial irradiation (PCI) is an option for patients achieving response to first-line therapy. The guidelines note that DLL3 IHC testing is not required for tarlatamab eligibility.
key_recommendations: Platinum-etoposide + atezolizumab or durvalumab 1L. Tarlatamab for previously treated ES-SCLC. Lurbinectedin alternative 2L. Topotecan for 2L+. CRS monitoring with tarlatamab. PCI for responders. Brain MRI surveillance. DLL3 testing not required for tarlatamab.
molecular_testing: Limited role for molecular testing in SCLC; DLL3 IHC (not required for tarlatamab); PD-L1 (not predictive in SCLC); consider comprehensive profiling for rare targetable alterations

text_summary: The NCCN Guidelines for Soft Tissue Sarcoma (Version 2.2025) encompass a broad group of mesenchymal malignancies with subtype-specific recommendations. Tazemetostat (EZH2 inhibitor) is recommended for metastatic or locally advanced epithelioid sarcoma with SMARCB1 (INI1) loss. Doxorubicin-based regimens remain the standard first-line for most advanced soft tissue sarcomas. Pazopanib is approved for non-adipocytic soft tissue sarcomas after chemotherapy. Trabectedin is approved for liposarcoma and leiomyosarcoma. Nirogacestat (gamma-secretase inhibitor) is approved for progressive desmoid tumors. The guidelines recommend INI1 IHC for suspected epithelioid sarcoma, MDM2/CDK4 FISH for well-differentiated/dedifferentiated liposarcoma, and SS18 (SYT) rearrangement testing for synovial sarcoma. Molecular profiling is increasingly important as subtype-specific targets are identified. Surgical resection remains the primary treatment for localized disease with wide margins and consideration of radiation therapy.
key_recommendations: Tazemetostat for INI1-negative epithelioid sarcoma. Doxorubicin-based 1L for most advanced STS. Pazopanib for non-adipocytic STS. Trabectedin for liposarcoma/leiomyosarcoma. Nirogacestat for desmoid tumors. INI1 IHC for epithelioid sarcoma. MDM2/CDK4 FISH for liposarcoma. Subtype-specific molecular testing.
molecular_testing: INI1 IHC (SMARCB1, epithelioid sarcoma), MDM2/CDK4 FISH (liposarcoma), SS18 rearrangement (synovial sarcoma), EZH2 mutation testing, comprehensive genomic profiling for rare subtypes

text_summary: The ESMO Clinical Practice Guidelines for Cholangiocarcinoma (2024) recommend comprehensive molecular profiling for all patients with advanced biliary tract cancers to identify actionable alterations. FGFR2 fusions/rearrangements (10-16% of intrahepatic cholangiocarcinoma) are targetable with pemigatinib, futibatinib, or infigratinib. IDH1 mutations (10-15% of iCCA) are targetable with ivosidenib (ClarIDHy trial). BRAF V600E mutations (3-5%) warrant dabrafenib plus trametinib. MSI-H/dMMR tumors qualify for pembrolizumab. HER2 amplification (5-15% of extrahepatic/gallbladder) may be addressed with trastuzumab-based therapy. First-line standard is gemcitabine plus cisplatin plus durvalumab (TOPAZ-1 trial: OS 12.8 vs 11.5 months). ESMO recommends NGS-based profiling (preferably including RNA sequencing for fusions) at diagnosis. The guidelines use the ESCAT framework to classify molecular targets, with FGFR2 fusions and IDH1 mutations as ESCAT tier I in cholangiocarcinoma.
key_recommendations: Comprehensive molecular profiling for all advanced biliary cancers. Gemcitabine + cisplatin + durvalumab 1L. FGFR2 fusion: pemigatinib/futibatinib. IDH1 mutant: ivosidenib. BRAF V600E: dabrafenib + trametinib. MSI-H: pembrolizumab. HER2-amplified: trastuzumab-based. ESCAT I: FGFR2 fusions, IDH1 mutations.
molecular_testing: NGS (DNA + RNA for fusions), FGFR2 fusions, IDH1/IDH2, BRAF V600E, MSI/MMR, HER2, NTRK fusions, TMB, BRCA1/2

text_summary: The ESMO Clinical Practice Guidelines for Endometrial Cancer (2024) incorporate the molecular classification system (TCGA-based ProMisE/TransPORTEC) into risk stratification and treatment decisions. Four molecular subtypes are recognized: POLE ultramutated (favorable prognosis, may omit adjuvant therapy), MSI-H/dMMR (immunotherapy responsive), p53-abnormal (poor prognosis, aggressive treatment), and no specific molecular profile (NSMP, intermediate). Dostarlimab plus carboplatin/paclitaxel is the new first-line standard for advanced/recurrent dMMR endometrial cancer based on the RUBY trial (PFS HR 0.28 in dMMR population). Lenvatinib plus pembrolizumab remains the standard for previously treated pMMR advanced endometrial cancer (KEYNOTE-775). MSI/MMR testing is mandatory at diagnosis. The guidelines recommend p53 IHC, POLE mutation analysis, and MSI/MMR testing for all endometrial cancers to enable molecular classification. HER2 testing is recommended for serous histology. Hormone receptor status guides endocrine therapy options.
key_recommendations: Molecular classification (POLE, MSI-H, p53-abn, NSMP) for all endometrial cancers. Dostarlimab + chemo for advanced dMMR. Lenvatinib + pembrolizumab for pretreated pMMR. MSI/MMR testing mandatory. POLE analysis for risk stratification. p53 IHC for prognostication. HER2 testing for serous histology.
molecular_testing: MSI/MMR (universal), POLE exonuclease domain, p53 IHC, HER2 (serous), ER/PR, comprehensive genomic profiling for advanced disease

text_summary: The ASCO Guideline on Management of MSI-H/dMMR Gastrointestinal and Other Solid Tumors (2024 update) provides comprehensive recommendations for the identification and treatment of MSI-H/dMMR cancers. Universal MSI/MMR testing is recommended for all colorectal, endometrial, gastric, and small intestinal cancers at diagnosis. Testing methods include IHC for MMR proteins (MLH1, MSH2, MSH6, PMS2), PCR-based MSI testing, and NGS-based MSI assessment. For metastatic MSI-H/dMMR CRC, pembrolizumab is the preferred first-line therapy (KEYNOTE-177). For advanced MSI-H/dMMR endometrial cancer, dostarlimab plus chemotherapy is recommended (RUBY). Nivolumab plus ipilimumab is an option for MSI-H/dMMR CRC. In non-CRC, non-endometrial MSI-H tumors, pembrolizumab (tissue-agnostic approval) is the standard. Lynch syndrome screening (MLH1 methylation and BRAF V600E testing to exclude sporadic MSI-H CRC) should follow MSI-H identification. Genetic counseling is mandatory for confirmed Lynch syndrome.
key_recommendations: Universal MSI/MMR testing for CRC, endometrial, gastric, small intestinal. Pembrolizumab 1L for MSI-H mCRC. Dostarlimab + chemo for advanced MSI-H endometrial. Nivolumab + ipilimumab for MSI-H CRC. Tissue-agnostic pembrolizumab for other MSI-H tumors. Lynch syndrome screening after MSI-H identification. Genetic counseling mandatory.
molecular_testing: IHC (MLH1, MSH2, MSH6, PMS2), PCR-based MSI, NGS-based MSI, MLH1 methylation, BRAF V600E (for Lynch screening), germline MMR gene testing

text_summary: The NCCN Genetic/Familial High-Risk Assessment: Breast, Ovarian, and Pancreatic Guidelines (Version 1.2025) provide comprehensive recommendations for germline genetic testing, risk management, and cancer prevention in individuals with hereditary cancer predisposition syndromes. BRCA1/2 testing criteria have been expanded to include all breast cancer patients diagnosed at age 65 or younger, all ovarian cancer patients regardless of family history, all pancreatic cancer patients, and metastatic prostate cancer patients. Multi-gene panel testing (including BRCA1/2, PALB2, ATM, CHEK2, RAD51C, RAD51D, BARD1, CDH1, TP53, PTEN, STK11) is preferred over sequential single-gene testing. Risk-reducing surgery recommendations include bilateral mastectomy for BRCA1/2 (50-85% lifetime breast cancer risk), risk-reducing salpingo-oophorectomy by age 35-40 for BRCA1 and 40-45 for BRCA2, and prophylactic gastrectomy for CDH1 carriers. Enhanced screening protocols are gene-specific.
key_recommendations: Expanded BRCA testing criteria. Multi-gene panel testing preferred. Risk-reducing mastectomy for BRCA1/2 carriers. RRSO by age 35-40 for BRCA1, 40-45 for BRCA2. Prophylactic gastrectomy for CDH1. PALB2 carriers: enhanced screening + consider risk-reducing surgery. ATM/CHEK2: enhanced screening. Cascade testing for at-risk relatives.
molecular_testing: Multi-gene germline panels (BRCA1/2, PALB2, ATM, CHEK2, RAD51C, RAD51D, BARD1, CDH1, TP53, PTEN, STK11, MLH1, MSH2, MSH6, PMS2, EPCAM)

text_summary: The FDA Tissue-Agnostic Approvals Summary (2025) consolidates the growing landscape of biomarker-driven, tumor-type-independent drug approvals. Current tissue-agnostic approvals include: pembrolizumab for MSI-H/dMMR solid tumors (2017, KEYNOTE-158/KEYNOTE-177), pembrolizumab for TMB-H (>=10 mut/Mb) solid tumors (2020, KEYNOTE-158), larotrectinib for NTRK fusion-positive solid tumors (2018, NAVIGATE/SCOUT), entrectinib for NTRK fusion-positive solid tumors (2019, STARTRK), dostarlimab for dMMR recurrent/advanced solid tumors (2022, GARNET), and dabrafenib plus trametinib for BRAF V600E-mutant solid tumors (2022). These approvals represent a paradigm shift from organ-of-origin to molecular-driven oncology. Common biomarker testing platforms include FoundationOne CDx (MSI, TMB, NTRK, BRAF), MSK-IMPACT, and Guardant360 CDx. The framework requires validated companion diagnostics and emphasizes that molecular profiling should be standard for all patients with advanced solid tumors lacking established treatment options.
key_recommendations: Pembrolizumab for MSI-H/dMMR and TMB-H solid tumors. Larotrectinib and entrectinib for NTRK fusion-positive. Dostarlimab for dMMR. Dabrafenib + trametinib for BRAF V600E. Comprehensive molecular profiling recommended for all advanced cancers. Validated companion diagnostics required. Molecular-first approach for refractory tumors.
molecular_testing: FoundationOne CDx (MSI, TMB, NTRK, BRAF), MSK-IMPACT, Guardant360 CDx, tissue-based NGS, liquid biopsy, IHC for MMR proteins

text_summary: The NCCN Guidelines for Myeloproliferative Neoplasms (Version 1.2025) provide recommendations for the diagnosis, risk stratification, and treatment of polycythemia vera (PV), essential thrombocythemia (ET), and primary myelofibrosis (PMF). Molecular testing for JAK2 V617F is the first-line diagnostic test, positive in approximately 95% of PV and 55-65% of ET and PMF. JAK2 V617F-negative patients should undergo testing for CALR (20-25% of ET/PMF) and MPL (3-5% of ET/PMF) mutations. Triple-negative MPN (JAK2/CALR/MPL wild-type) has adverse prognosis and should prompt NGS for alternative driver mutations and exclusion of other diagnoses. Risk stratification in PMF uses the Dynamic International Prognostic Scoring System (DIPSS) and MIPSS70 (incorporating molecular data including ASXL1, EZH2, SRSF2, IDH1/2 mutations). Ruxolitinib is the standard JAK inhibitor for intermediate-2/high-risk myelofibrosis and hydroxyurea-resistant/intolerant PV. Fedratinib and pacritinib are second-line JAK inhibitors. Momelotinib is approved for myelofibrosis with anemia. Allogeneic stem cell transplant remains the only curative option for myelofibrosis. Interferon-alpha (ropeginterferon) is an option for low-risk PV and ET.
key_recommendations: JAK2 V617F testing first-line for all suspected MPN. CALR and MPL testing for JAK2-negative. DIPSS/MIPSS70 risk stratification in PMF. Ruxolitinib for intermediate-2/high-risk MF and HU-resistant PV. Fedratinib/pacritinib for second-line MF. Momelotinib for MF with anemia. Ropeginterferon for low-risk PV. Allogeneic SCT for eligible high-risk MF patients.
molecular_testing: JAK2 V617F, CALR, MPL, NGS myeloid panel (ASXL1, EZH2, SRSF2, IDH1/2, TP53, RUNX1, U2AF1, SF3B1), cytogenetics, bone marrow biopsy

text_summary: The NCCN Guidelines for Cervical Cancer (Version 1.2025) integrate molecular biomarkers into the treatment algorithm for advanced and recurrent disease. PD-L1 CPS testing is recommended for recurrent or metastatic cervical cancer to guide immunotherapy with pembrolizumab. The KEYNOTE-826 trial established pembrolizumab plus chemotherapy (with or without bevacizumab) as the first-line standard for persistent, recurrent, or metastatic cervical cancer with CPS >=1 (OS 24.4 vs 16.5 months). Tisotumab vedotin (tissue factor-directed ADC) is approved for recurrent or metastatic cervical cancer after prior chemotherapy. MSI-H/dMMR and TMB-H testing enable tissue-agnostic immunotherapy access. NTRK fusion testing is recommended. HPV screening (primary hrHPV testing or co-testing with cytology) is the cornerstone of cervical cancer prevention, with HPV types 16 and 18 conferring highest risk. The guidelines emphasize that concurrent chemoradiation with cisplatin remains the standard for locally advanced cervical cancer, with pembrolizumab maintenance after chemoradiation showing benefit in the KEYNOTE-A18 trial for high-risk locally advanced disease.
key_recommendations: Pembrolizumab + chemo +/- bevacizumab for CPS>=1 recurrent/metastatic. Tisotumab vedotin for pretreated disease. Concurrent chemoradiation for locally advanced. Pembrolizumab maintenance after chemoRT for high-risk (KEYNOTE-A18). HPV screening for prevention. MSI/TMB testing for tissue-agnostic options.
molecular_testing: PD-L1 CPS (22C3), HPV genotyping, MSI/MMR, TMB, NTRK fusions, comprehensive genomic profiling for recurrent disease

text_summary: The NCCN Guidelines for Hepatocellular Carcinoma (Version 1.2025) recommend atezolizumab plus bevacizumab as the preferred first-line systemic therapy for unresectable HCC based on the IMbrave150 trial (OS 19.2 months vs 13.4 months for sorafenib). Tremelimumab plus durvalumab (HIMALAYA trial STRIDE regimen) is an alternative first-line option, particularly for patients with contraindications to bevacizumab such as variceal bleeding risk. Sorafenib and lenvatinib remain alternative first-line TKIs. Second-line options include cabozantinib, regorafenib, and ramucirumab (for AFP >=400 ng/mL). HCC surveillance in high-risk populations (cirrhosis, chronic HBV) uses ultrasound and AFP every 6 months. Barcelona Clinic Liver Cancer (BCLC) staging guides treatment selection across surgical, locoregional, and systemic modalities. Molecular profiling is recommended for advanced HCC to identify MSI-H/dMMR, TMB-H, and NTRK fusions for tissue-agnostic therapies. The guidelines emphasize the importance of liver function assessment (Child-Pugh score) in treatment selection, as immunotherapy and TKIs are indicated only for Child-Pugh A patients.
key_recommendations: Atezolizumab + bevacizumab preferred 1L for unresectable HCC. Tremelimumab + durvalumab alternative 1L. Sorafenib/lenvatinib as TKI alternatives. Cabozantinib/regorafenib for 2L. Ramucirumab for AFP>=400. HCC surveillance with US + AFP q6 months. BCLC staging for treatment selection. Child-Pugh A required for systemic therapy.
molecular_testing: AFP (surveillance and ramucirumab eligibility), MSI/MMR, TMB, NTRK fusions, comprehensive genomic profiling (limited actionable targets in HCC), hepatitis B/C status

text_summary: The NCCN Guidelines for Gastrointestinal Stromal Tumors (GIST, Version 1.2025) mandate KIT and PDGFRA mutation testing at diagnosis for all GISTs to guide targeted therapy selection. Approximately 80% of GISTs harbor KIT mutations (exon 11 most common, then exon 9, 13, 17), 5-10% have PDGFRA mutations (D842V being the most common), and 10-15% are KIT/PDGFRA wild-type. Imatinib 400 mg daily is the standard first-line for KIT-mutant and most PDGFRA-mutant advanced GIST, with KIT exon 9 mutations requiring imatinib 800 mg. PDGFRA D842V mutations are resistant to imatinib but sensitive to avapritinib (NAVIGATOR trial: ORR 88%). Sunitinib is the standard second-line after imatinib failure. Regorafenib and ripretinib are approved for later lines. Adjuvant imatinib for 3 years is standard for high-risk resected GIST (based on tumor size, mitotic rate, location, and KIT mutation type). The guidelines emphasize that mutation-specific therapy selection is critical in GIST and that SDH-deficient GIST (pediatric/Carney triad) has distinct biology requiring alternative management.
key_recommendations: KIT/PDGFRA mutation testing mandatory at diagnosis. Imatinib 400mg for KIT-mutant GIST. Imatinib 800mg for KIT exon 9. Avapritinib for PDGFRA D842V. Sunitinib 2L after imatinib. Regorafenib 3L. Ripretinib 4L. Adjuvant imatinib 3 years for high-risk resected GIST.
molecular_testing: KIT exon 9, 11, 13, 17 mutations, PDGFRA exon 12, 14, 18 mutations (especially D842V), SDH (SDHA/B/C/D for wild-type GIST), NF1, BRAF, RAS for wild-type GIST

text_summary: The NCCN Guidelines for Malignant Pleural Mesothelioma (Version 1.2025) recommend nivolumab plus ipilimumab as the preferred first-line systemic therapy for unresectable malignant pleural mesothelioma based on the CheckMate-743 trial (OS 18.1 months vs 14.1 months for pemetrexed/cisplatin). This benefit was most pronounced in non-epithelioid histology (sarcomatoid/biphasic). Pemetrexed plus cisplatin or carboplatin with or without bevacizumab remains an alternative first-line option, particularly for epithelioid histology. BAP1 loss by IHC is a diagnostic biomarker distinguishing mesothelioma from benign mesothelial proliferations and may have prognostic significance. CDKN2A homozygous deletion detected by FISH is both a diagnostic marker and potential therapeutic target for CDK4/6 inhibitors under investigation. The guidelines recommend molecular profiling for NF2, BAP1, CDKN2A, and other alterations. Surgical approaches (extrapleural pneumonectomy or pleurectomy/decortication) are considered in select patients with epithelioid histology and good performance status.
key_recommendations: Nivolumab + ipilimumab preferred 1L for unresectable mesothelioma. Pemetrexed + platinum alternative 1L. BAP1 IHC for diagnosis. CDKN2A FISH for diagnosis and potential CDK4/6i target. NF2 loss common. Surgery for select epithelioid patients. Histologic subtyping critical for treatment selection.
molecular_testing: BAP1 IHC, CDKN2A FISH (homozygous deletion), NF2, comprehensive genomic profiling, PD-L1 (limited utility for treatment selection), molecular profiling for clinical trial eligibility

text_summary: The NCCN Guidelines for Testicular Cancer (Version 1.2025) recommend serum tumor marker monitoring with AFP, beta-hCG, and LDH as essential components of diagnosis, staging, treatment response assessment, and post-treatment surveillance. The International Germ Cell Consensus Classification (IGCCC) risk stratification uses serum markers to classify patients into good, intermediate, and poor prognosis groups for metastatic non-seminoma: good risk (AFP <1000, hCG <5000, LDH <1.5x ULN), intermediate risk (AFP 1000-10000, hCG 5000-50000, LDH 1.5-10x ULN), and poor risk (AFP >10000, hCG >50000, LDH >10x ULN, or non-pulmonary visceral metastases). BEP (bleomycin, etoposide, cisplatin) is the standard chemotherapy for good/intermediate risk, with 3 or 4 cycles respectively. Incomplete marker decline during chemotherapy or rising markers after treatment indicates treatment failure. Surveillance protocols for stage I disease use serial markers and imaging to detect early relapse. The guidelines emphasize that germ cell tumors are among the most curable solid tumors, with overall cure rates exceeding 95% for early-stage and 70-80% for advanced disease.
key_recommendations: AFP, beta-hCG, LDH at diagnosis and during treatment. IGCCC risk stratification (good/intermediate/poor). BEP chemotherapy standard for good/intermediate risk. EP x4 for intermediate/poor. Marker kinetics guide treatment response. Active surveillance for stage I. RPLND for residual masses with normal markers. Cure rates >95% early-stage.
molecular_testing: AFP, beta-hCG, LDH (mandatory), i(12p) cytogenetics (diagnostic), comprehensive profiling for refractory/late-relapse cases, KIT mutations in seminoma (limited utility)

text_summary: The ESMO Clinical Practice Guidelines for Metastatic Non-Small Cell Lung Cancer (2024) emphasize the central role of molecular profiling in treatment selection and provide the ESCAT framework for prioritizing genomic alterations. ESCAT Tier I alterations in NSCLC include EGFR mutations (osimertinib), ALK fusions (alectinib, lorlatinib), ROS1 fusions (crizotinib, entrectinib), BRAF V600E (dabrafenib + trametinib), NTRK fusions (larotrectinib, entrectinib), RET fusions (selpercatinib), MET exon 14 skipping (capmatinib, tepotinib), and KRAS G12C (sotorasib, adagrasib). ESMO recommends upfront broad NGS panel testing rather than sequential single-gene testing to minimize tissue usage and turnaround time. For non-oncogene-addicted NSCLC, PD-L1 expression guides immunotherapy: pembrolizumab monotherapy for TPS>=50%, pembrolizumab plus chemotherapy for TPS 1-49%, and chemotherapy-immunotherapy combinations for TPS<1%. ESMO provides specific sequencing recommendations after resistance to first-line targeted therapy, including repeat biopsy and molecular profiling to identify resistance mechanisms. Liquid biopsy is endorsed for EGFR T790M detection and when tissue is unavailable.
key_recommendations: Broad NGS panel testing upfront for all advanced NSCLC. ESCAT I: EGFR, ALK, ROS1, BRAF V600E, NTRK, RET, MET ex14, KRAS G12C. PD-L1-guided immunotherapy for non-driver NSCLC. Repeat biopsy at resistance. Liquid biopsy for T790M and when tissue unavailable. Sequencing recommendations after targeted therapy failure.
molecular_testing: NGS panel (EGFR, ALK, ROS1, BRAF, NTRK, RET, MET, KRAS, ERBB2), PD-L1 IHC, liquid biopsy (ctDNA), RNA-based sequencing for fusions

text_summary: The ESMO Clinical Practice Guidelines for Metastatic Breast Cancer (2024) provide comprehensive recommendations integrating molecular subtyping into treatment algorithms. For HR-positive/HER2-negative metastatic breast cancer, CDK4/6 inhibitor (ribociclib preferred based on OS data from MONALEESA trials) plus aromatase inhibitor is the standard first-line. ESR1 mutation testing by ctDNA is recommended at progression on aromatase inhibitor to guide switch to fulvestrant. PIK3CA mutation testing guides alpelisib use. AKT pathway alteration (PIK3CA/AKT1/PTEN) testing guides capivasertib use (CAPItello-291). For HER2-positive disease, ESMO endorses the DESTINY-Breast03 data establishing T-DXd as preferred second-line. HER2-low (IHC 1+ or 2+/FISH-negative) is recognized as a treatable subgroup with T-DXd (DESTINY-Breast04). For TNBC, pembrolizumab plus chemotherapy is standard neoadjuvant (KEYNOTE-522), and PD-L1 CPS testing guides first-line immunotherapy in the metastatic setting. Germline BRCA testing is recommended for all metastatic breast cancer patients. ESMO emphasizes molecular tumor boards for interpretation of complex genomic profiles.
key_recommendations: CDK4/6i + AI for HR+/HER2- metastatic (ribociclib preferred). ESR1 ctDNA testing at AI progression. PIK3CA testing for alpelisib. AKT pathway testing for capivasertib. T-DXd for HER2-positive 2L and HER2-low. Pembrolizumab + chemo for TNBC neoadjuvant. BRCA testing for all metastatic breast cancer.
molecular_testing: ER, PR, HER2 IHC/FISH, ESR1 (ctDNA), PIK3CA, AKT1/PTEN, germline BRCA1/2, PD-L1 CPS (TNBC), Oncotype DX/MammaPrint (early-stage), comprehensive profiling for advanced disease

text_summary: The ESMO Clinical Practice Guidelines for Gastric/GEJ Cancer (2024 update) mandate parallel biomarker testing at diagnosis for HER2, PD-L1 CPS, MSI/MMR, and CLDN18.2 to avoid treatment delays. For HER2-positive advanced gastric cancer, trastuzumab plus fluoropyrimidine/platinum remains the first-line standard, with T-DXd established as preferred second-line (DESTINY-Gastric01/02). For HER2-negative disease, treatment selection is biomarker-driven: CLDN18.2-positive patients receive zolbetuximab plus CAPOX or mFOLFOX6 (SPOTLIGHT/GLOW), PD-L1 CPS>=5 patients receive nivolumab plus chemotherapy (CheckMate-649), and MSI-H patients may receive pembrolizumab (tissue-agnostic). ESMO classifies HER2 amplification, CLDN18.2 expression, and MSI-H as ESCAT tier I in gastric cancer. Comprehensive molecular profiling is recommended for advanced disease to identify NTRK fusions, BRAF V600E, and other actionable alterations in the minority of patients with these genomic changes. FGFR2 amplification is an emerging biomarker with bemarituzumab showing promise in the FIGHT trial.
key_recommendations: Parallel HER2, PD-L1 CPS, MSI, CLDN18.2 testing at diagnosis. Trastuzumab + chemo for HER2-positive. Zolbetuximab + chemo for CLDN18.2-positive/HER2-negative. Nivolumab + chemo for CPS>=5. T-DXd for HER2-positive 2L. Pembrolizumab for MSI-H. FGFR2 amplification emerging target.
molecular_testing: HER2 IHC/FISH, CLDN18.2 IHC, PD-L1 CPS, MSI/MMR, FGFR2 amplification, NTRK fusions, BRAF V600E, comprehensive genomic profiling

text_summary: The ESMO Clinical Practice Guidelines for Renal Cell Carcinoma (2024) stratify first-line treatment by IMDC (International Metastatic RCC Database Consortium) risk category rather than molecular biomarkers. For intermediate/poor-risk clear cell RCC, nivolumab plus ipilimumab (CheckMate-214: 4-year OS 53%) or pembrolizumab plus lenvatinib (CLEAR: PFS 23.9 months) are preferred. For favorable-risk, pembrolizumab plus axitinib or single-agent TKI (sunitinib, pazopanib) are recommended. ESMO acknowledges that predictive biomarkers for immunotherapy in RCC remain an unmet need, with PD-L1, TMB, and gene expression signatures showing inconsistent utility. VHL mutation/methylation status does not guide systemic therapy selection in sporadic RCC but is diagnostic for VHL disease. Belzutifan (HIF-2alpha inhibitor) is approved for VHL disease-associated RCC. For non-clear cell RCC (papillary, chromophobe, collecting duct), ESMO recommends molecular profiling to identify potential targets including MET alterations in papillary RCC. Cabozantinib is active across clear cell and non-clear cell histologies. Sarcomatoid differentiation is associated with higher PD-L1 and better immunotherapy response.
key_recommendations: IMDC risk-stratified first-line treatment. Nivolumab + ipilimumab or pembrolizumab + lenvatinib for intermediate/poor risk. Pembrolizumab + axitinib for favorable risk. Belzutifan for VHL disease. Molecular profiling for non-clear cell. Cabozantinib active across histologies. Sarcomatoid features predict immunotherapy response.
molecular_testing: VHL (germline for VHL disease), MET alterations (papillary RCC), PD-L1 (limited utility), molecular profiling for non-clear cell, FH (hereditary leiomyomatosis/RCC), comprehensive genomic profiling for refractory disease

text_summary: The ASCO Guideline on Germline Testing for Patients with Cancer (2024 update) provides comprehensive recommendations for when and how to implement hereditary cancer predisposition testing across tumor types. ASCO recommends germline multi-gene panel testing for all patients with ovarian cancer (any age/histology), pancreatic cancer (any age), metastatic prostate cancer, breast cancer diagnosed at age 65 or younger or triple-negative at any age, and colorectal cancer diagnosed under 50 or with MSI-H/dMMR tumors. Universal germline testing for all cancer patients is endorsed as an aspirational goal to identify hereditary syndromes in patients who would otherwise be missed by family history-based criteria alone. Minimum recommended panels should include BRCA1/2, PALB2, ATM, CHEK2, MLH1, MSH2, MSH6, PMS2, and TP53, with tumor-type-specific gene additions. All germline testing must include pre-test informed consent and access to genetic counseling for results disclosure. Incidental findings of pathogenic variants in non-cancer genes (e.g., cardiac, neurological) should follow ACMG guidelines for secondary findings. Cascade testing of at-risk relatives is strongly recommended for all identified pathogenic variant carriers.
key_recommendations: Germline panel testing for ovarian, pancreatic, metastatic prostate, breast <=65, TNBC. Universal germline testing aspirational goal. Minimum panels: BRCA1/2, PALB2, ATM, CHEK2, MMR genes, TP53. Pre-test consent and genetic counseling mandatory. Cascade testing for relatives. ACMG guidelines for secondary findings.
molecular_testing: Multi-gene germline panels (BRCA1/2, PALB2, ATM, CHEK2, RAD51C/D, BARD1, CDH1, TP53, PTEN, STK11, MLH1, MSH2, MSH6, PMS2, EPCAM, BRIP1, NF1, RB1, APC, MUTYH)

text_summary: The ASCO Guideline on Liquid Biopsy in Advanced Solid Tumors (2024) provides evidence-based recommendations for the clinical use of circulating tumor DNA (ctDNA) testing across cancer types and clinical scenarios. ASCO endorses ctDNA testing as a complement to tissue-based molecular profiling when tissue is insufficient, unavailable, or when rapid results are needed for treatment decisions. FDA-cleared ctDNA assays (Guardant360 CDx, FoundationOne Liquid CDx) are validated companion diagnostics for specific drug-biomarker indications. In NSCLC, ctDNA testing is recommended for EGFR T790M resistance mutation detection at progression on first/second-generation TKIs. In breast cancer, ctDNA ESR1 mutation testing at progression on aromatase inhibitor guides endocrine therapy selection. For MRD detection in solid tumors, tumor-informed ctDNA assays (Signatera, RaDaR) show promise for guiding adjuvant therapy decisions in CRC and bladder cancer, though ASCO considers MRD-directed treatment de-escalation or escalation as investigational. Key limitations include lower sensitivity than tissue for certain alterations, potential confounding by clonal hematopoiesis of indeterminate potential (CHIP), and variable performance across tumor types based on tumor shedding rates.
key_recommendations: ctDNA complements tissue profiling when tissue unavailable. FDA-cleared assays for specific companion diagnostic indications. EGFR T790M detection by ctDNA in NSCLC. ESR1 ctDNA testing at AI progression in breast. MRD-directed therapy investigational. CHIP filtering essential. ctDNA kinetics as emerging response biomarker.
molecular_testing: Guardant360 CDx, FoundationOne Liquid CDx, Signatera (tumor-informed MRD), RaDaR, ddPCR for specific mutations, methylation-based cfDNA assays (GRAIL Galleri for multi-cancer detection)

text_summary: The CAP/AMP (College of American Pathologists / Association for Molecular Pathology) Molecular Testing Standards (2024 update) provide comprehensive guidelines for validation, quality assurance, and reporting of molecular oncology testing in clinical laboratories. Key standards include minimum requirements for NGS panel validation (analytical sensitivity >=99% for SNVs at >=5% VAF, >=95% for indels), proficiency testing participation, and structured molecular pathology reporting. CAP/AMP recommends that all clinical NGS laboratories participate in external proficiency testing programs and implement internal quality metrics including sequencing coverage depth (minimum 500x for targeted panels), variant allele frequency accuracy, and turnaround time targets (<=14 business days for solid tumors, <=7 days for hematologic malignancies). Variant classification follows the AMP/ASCO/CAP joint consensus guidelines using a four-tier system: Tier I (strong clinical significance, FDA-approved therapies), Tier II (potential clinical significance, clinical trial eligibility), Tier III (unknown clinical significance), and Tier IV (benign/likely benign). The guidelines emphasize standardized reporting elements including specimen adequacy assessment, tested genes and regions, detected variants with tier classification, and therapeutic implications with evidence level.
key_recommendations: NGS validation: >=99% sensitivity for SNVs at >=5% VAF. Proficiency testing mandatory. Minimum 500x coverage for targeted panels. AMP/ASCO/CAP 4-tier variant classification. Structured molecular reports. TAT <=14 days solid tumors, <=7 days hematologic. External QA participation. Specimen adequacy documentation.
molecular_testing: NGS panel validation standards, variant calling pipelines (SNV, indels, CNV, fusions), quality metrics (coverage, VAF accuracy), proficiency testing programs (CAP, EMQN), reporting templates

text_summary: The NCCN Genetic/Familial High-Risk Assessment: Colorectal Guidelines (Version 1.2025) provide recommendations for Lynch syndrome identification, Li-Fraumeni syndrome, familial adenomatous polyposis (FAP), and other hereditary colorectal cancer syndromes. Universal tumor testing for MSI/MMR is recommended for all newly diagnosed colorectal cancers regardless of age or family history, serving as a screening tool for Lynch syndrome. MLH1-deficient tumors should undergo reflex BRAF V600E and/or MLH1 methylation testing to distinguish sporadic MSI-H (MLH1 methylation/BRAF V600E-positive) from Lynch syndrome. Germline testing for MLH1, MSH2, MSH6, PMS2, and EPCAM is indicated when Lynch syndrome is suspected. APC gene testing is recommended for classic FAP (>100 adenomas) and attenuated FAP. MUTYH biallelic testing is indicated for polyposis without APC mutation. The guidelines provide colonoscopy surveillance schedules gene-specific to each syndrome: Lynch syndrome carriers should begin colonoscopy at age 20-25 (or 2-5 years before youngest CRC in family) with repeat every 1-2 years. Risk-reducing hysterectomy and salpingo-oophorectomy are discussed for female Lynch syndrome carriers.
key_recommendations: Universal MSI/MMR testing for all CRC. MLH1 methylation/BRAF reflex testing. Germline MMR gene testing for suspected Lynch. APC testing for FAP. MUTYH for non-APC polyposis. Colonoscopy q1-2 years for Lynch starting age 20-25. Risk-reducing surgery for Lynch females. Cascade testing for relatives.
molecular_testing: MSI/MMR (universal CRC), MLH1 methylation, BRAF V600E, germline panels (MLH1, MSH2, MSH6, PMS2, EPCAM, APC, MUTYH, STK11, SMAD4, BMPR1A, POLD1, POLE, GREM1, AXIN2)

text_summary: The WHO Classification of Haematolymphoid Tumours (5th Edition, 2022/2024) integrates molecular genetics into the diagnostic framework for hematologic malignancies, establishing molecular-defined entities alongside morphologic and immunophenotypic criteria. In AML, the WHO defines specific genetic subtypes including AML with NPM1 mutation, AML with CEBPA mutation, AML with PML-RARA (APL), AML with RUNX1-RUNX1T1, AML with CBFB-MYH11, AML with KMT2A rearrangement, and AML with BCR-ABL1. TP53-mutated AML is recognized as a distinct entity with uniformly poor prognosis. In myelodysplastic neoplasms, SF3B1 mutation defines MDS with low blasts and ring sideroblasts as a favorable entity. In lymphoid neoplasms, specific genetic alterations define diagnostic categories: cyclin D1-positive mantle cell lymphoma (t(11;14)), Burkitt lymphoma (MYC rearrangement), and ALK-positive anaplastic large cell lymphoma. The WHO classification emphasizes that molecular testing is now essential for accurate diagnosis and classification of most hematologic malignancies, and that entities defined by genetic alterations take precedence over morphologic categorization when discrepancies arise.
key_recommendations: Molecular-defined entities for AML (NPM1, CEBPA, PML-RARA, etc.). TP53-mutated AML as distinct poor-prognosis entity. SF3B1-mutated MDS as favorable entity. Genetic alterations define lymphoma subtypes. Molecular testing essential for classification. Genetic entities take precedence over morphology when discrepant.
molecular_testing: Karyotype/FISH (mandatory), targeted NGS myeloid panel (NPM1, FLT3, CEBPA, TP53, IDH1/2, RUNX1, ASXL1, SF3B1, SRSF2, U2AF1, DNMT3A, TET2, EZH2), RT-PCR for fusions (PML-RARA, BCR-ABL1), flow cytometry immunophenotyping

text_summary: The ESMO Precision Medicine Scale (ESCAT 2.0, 2024 update) provides a revised tiered framework for classifying molecular targets based on clinical evidence strength, specifically designed to guide reimbursement and access decisions across European healthcare systems. ESCAT 2.0 introduces sub-tiers: Tier IA (standard of care based on prospective trials), Tier IB (strong evidence from subgroup analyses or basket trials), Tier IC (tissue-agnostic approvals with heterogeneous evidence across tumor types), Tier IIA (prospective non-randomized evidence), Tier IIB (retrospective data), and Tiers III-IV (cross-tumor type evidence and preclinical only). Key Tier IA classifications include EGFR mutations in NSCLC, HER2 amplification in breast/gastric, BRAF V600E in melanoma, ALK fusions in NSCLC, BRCA mutations in ovarian/breast/prostate/pancreatic, and FGFR2 fusions in cholangiocarcinoma. Tier IC includes tissue-agnostic approvals for MSI-H/dMMR, TMB-H, and NTRK fusions. ESCAT 2.0 emphasizes that Tier IA-IB alterations should be universally accessible through standard molecular testing, while Tier II-III may justify clinical trial enrollment. The framework has been adopted by multiple European countries for precision medicine reimbursement decisions.
key_recommendations: ESCAT 2.0 sub-tiers: IA (SOC), IB (strong basket/subgroup), IC (tissue-agnostic), IIA/IIB (investigational). Universal access for Tier IA-IB recommended. Tier II-III warrant trial enrollment. Guides reimbursement decisions. Standardizes cross-country precision medicine access in Europe.
molecular_testing: Comprehensive NGS mandatory for all advanced cancers. Minimum: tumor-type-specific panels. Optimal: DNA+RNA comprehensive profiling. Liquid biopsy when tissue unavailable. Molecular tumor board review for Tier II-IV alterations.

text_summary: The NCCN Supportive Care Guidelines for Antiemesis and Hematopoietic Growth Factors (Version 1.2025) provide evidence-based recommendations for managing chemotherapy-induced nausea/vomiting (CINV) and febrile neutropenia risk based on treatment regimen emetogenicity and myelosuppression potential. For highly emetogenic chemotherapy (HEC, >90% emesis risk: cisplatin, AC regimen, cyclophosphamide >1500 mg/m2), a four-drug antiemetic regimen is recommended: NK1 receptor antagonist (aprepitant, fosaprepitant, or netupitant) plus 5-HT3 receptor antagonist (ondansetron, granisetron, palonosetron) plus dexamethasone plus olanzapine. For moderately emetogenic chemotherapy (MEC), a three-drug regimen without olanzapine is standard. G-CSF (filgrastim, pegfilgrastim, biosimilars) prophylaxis is recommended when febrile neutropenia risk exceeds 20% from the chemotherapy regimen. The guidelines incorporate pharmacogenomic considerations: UGT1A1 polymorphisms affect irinotecan metabolism and toxicity, DPYD testing is recommended before fluoropyrimidine therapy to prevent fatal toxicity from dihydropyrimidine dehydrogenase deficiency, and TPMT/NUDT15 testing is recommended before thiopurine therapy. These pharmacogenomic tests exemplify precision supportive care.
key_recommendations: 4-drug antiemetic for HEC (NK1RA + 5-HT3RA + dexamethasone + olanzapine). 3-drug for MEC. G-CSF when FN risk >20%. DPYD testing before fluoropyrimidines. UGT1A1 testing before irinotecan. TPMT/NUDT15 before thiopurines. Biosimilar G-CSF acceptable. Dose modifications based on pharmacogenomics.
molecular_testing: DPYD genotyping (before fluoropyrimidines), UGT1A1 (before irinotecan), TPMT/NUDT15 (before thiopurines), pharmacogenomic panel testing

text_summary: The FDA Tissue-Agnostic Approvals Comprehensive Summary (2025 update) details the current landscape and regulatory framework for biomarker-driven, tumor-type-independent drug approvals in oncology. As of 2025, there are six FDA-approved tissue-agnostic indications: pembrolizumab for MSI-H/dMMR solid tumors (May 2017, KEYNOTE-158), pembrolizumab for TMB-H >=10 mut/Mb solid tumors (June 2020, KEYNOTE-158), larotrectinib for NTRK fusion-positive solid tumors (November 2018, NAVIGATE/SCOUT), entrectinib for NTRK fusion-positive solid tumors (August 2019, STARTRK-1/2/ALKA), dostarlimab for dMMR recurrent/advanced solid tumors (August 2021, GARNET), and dabrafenib plus trametinib for BRAF V600E-mutant unresectable/metastatic solid tumors (June 2022). The FDA has issued guidance on developing tissue-agnostic therapies, requiring demonstration of activity across at least 3 tumor types, durable responses, and validated companion diagnostics. Emerging tissue-agnostic candidates include selpercatinib for RET fusion-positive solid tumors and therapies targeting HER2 mutations across tumor types. The framework represents a fundamental shift from organ-of-origin to molecular-driven oncology treatment paradigms.
key_recommendations: Six tissue-agnostic approvals as of 2025. FDA guidance for tissue-agnostic development. Activity across >=3 tumor types required. Validated companion diagnostics mandatory. Emerging candidates: selpercatinib (RET), pan-HER2. Molecular profiling standard for refractory cancers. Real-world evidence supporting tissue-agnostic efficacy.
molecular_testing: MSI/MMR (IHC, PCR, NGS), TMB (FoundationOne CDx, >=10 mut/Mb), NTRK fusions (NGS DNA+RNA, IHC screening), BRAF V600E (NGS, IHC VE1), companion diagnostic requirements for each indication

text_summary: The NCCN Guidelines for Acute Myeloid Leukemia (Version 2.2025) expand molecular profiling recommendations with emphasis on rapid turnaround time for actionable mutations. FLT3 mutation testing (both ITD and TKD D835) must be available within 24-48 hours to guide midostaurin addition to induction chemotherapy. The 2022 European LeukemiaNet (ELN) risk classification, endorsed by NCCN, integrates molecular markers into three risk categories: favorable (t(8;21), inv(16), NPM1-mutant without FLT3-ITD, biallelic CEBPA), intermediate (NPM1-wild-type without adverse features, t(9;11)), and adverse (complex karyotype, TP53, ASXL1, BCOR, EZH2, RUNX1, SRSF2, SF3B1, U2AF1, ZRSR2 in NPM1-WT/FLT3-ITD-negative context). Venetoclax plus azacitidine has transformed treatment for elderly/unfit patients, with IDH1/2 mutations predicting particularly favorable response. Menin inhibitors (revumenib) are emerging for KMT2A-rearranged and NPM1-mutant relapsed AML. MRD assessment by multiparameter flow cytometry (sensitivity 10^-3 to 10^-4) or NPM1 RT-qPCR (sensitivity 10^-5) guides post-remission therapy decisions including transplant indication.
key_recommendations: FLT3 results within 24-48 hours mandatory. ELN 2022 risk classification. Venetoclax + azacitidine for unfit patients. IDH1/2 mutations predict venetoclax response. Menin inhibitors for KMT2A-rearranged/NPM1-mutant R/R. Gilteritinib/quizartinib for FLT3-mutant R/R. MRD guides transplant decisions. TP53 mutant: clinical trials.
molecular_testing: Rapid FLT3-ITD/TKD (24-48h), NPM1, IDH1, IDH2, TP53, CEBPA, RUNX1, ASXL1, KMT2A rearrangements, cytogenetics/FISH, MRD by flow and/or molecular (NPM1 qPCR), comprehensive myeloid NGS panel

text_summary: The NCCN Guidelines for Chronic Myeloid Leukemia (Version 2.2025) refine treatment selection and monitoring milestones with updated TKI options. First-line TKI selection considers CML risk scores (Sokal, EUTOS long-term survival), comorbidities, and patient preference: imatinib (generic available, well-tolerated, lowest rate of deep molecular response), dasatinib (faster cytogenetic/molecular response, risk of pleural effusion), nilotinib (higher MMR rates, cardiovascular risk), bosutinib (GI side effects, less cardiovascular risk than nilotinib), and asciminib (STAMP inhibitor, ASC4FIRST trial showing superior MMR at 48 weeks). Response milestones at 3 months (BCR-ABL1 <=10% IS or complete cytogenetic response), 12 months (BCR-ABL1 <=1% IS or MMR), and 18 months (BCR-ABL1 <=0.1% IS, MMR) guide treatment decisions. Treatment-free remission (TFR) is an achievable goal for patients maintaining MR4 or deeper for >=2 years, with approximately 40-60% of patients maintaining molecular remission after TKI discontinuation. Monthly BCR-ABL1 monitoring during the first year of TFR is critical, as molecular relapse most commonly occurs within 6 months of stopping TKI.
key_recommendations: TKI selection based on risk scores and comorbidities. Asciminib emerging as first-line option (ASC4FIRST). Milestone responses at 3/12/18 months guide decisions. ABL1 mutation analysis at failure/suboptimal response. TFR after sustained MR4 for >=2 years. Monthly monitoring during first year of TFR. Ponatinib/asciminib for T315I.
molecular_testing: BCR-ABL1 RT-qPCR (IS scale) every 3 months, ABL1 kinase domain mutation analysis (Sanger/NGS) at failure, cytogenetics at diagnosis and for monitoring, BCR-ABL1 transcript type (e13a2 vs e14a2)
